coredata.dc:title: Multiple confinement-limited activation and defect effect co-triggered the ultra-long lifetime of carbon dots

Jiang, Qin

He, Jiangling
coredata.dc:description: The luminescence process of carbon dots (CDs) is typically influenced by spin-forbidden transition, triplet exciton quenching, and non-radiative transition. Thus, it faces great challenges to achieve efficient and ultra-long lifetime afterglow for CDs. The commonly employed strategy to obtain efficient afterglow performance of CDs is to encapsulate CDs into a rigid matrix to protect the afterglow signal from quenching. Generally, the ultra-long lifetime and excellent luminescent properties of inorganic RTP (room temperature phosphorescence) nanomaterials attribute to the slow release of electrons trapped in defect states of the surfaces. This capture-release mechanism has advanced afterglow lifetime of CDs towards longer levels via the design of defect states. Anchoring CDs onto a rigid matrix can preserve the rigid structure for the restrictive effect, and also can create structural defects. Herein, as-prepared RHCDs@SiO2 composite can exhibit an ultra-long afterglow lifetime of 7.76 s, which is the highest value achieved for CDs in silica matrix to date. This composite is also successfully applied in anti-counterfeiting and cell imaging fields. The synergistic multiple confinement effect and defect effect from the composite provides a universal strategy to design and achieve the ultra-long afterglow lifetime of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Multiple confinement-limited activation
Defect effect
Ultra-long lifetime

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of RHCDs@SiO2. Inset: particle size distribution. (b) and (c) TEM images of RHCDs@SiO2. Inset: HRTEM images. (d) Particle size distribution of CDs in RHCDs@SiO2. (e) The FTIR spectra and (f) XRD patterns of RHCDs@SiO2 before and after the calcination.
(a) XPS curve of RHCDs@SiO2. High-resolution (b) C1s, (c) O1s and (d) Si2p XPS results for RHCDs@SiO2.
The photographs of RHCDs@SiO2 under (a) 254 nm and (c) 365 nm UV lamp excitation on or off. The Phos. spectrum of RHCDs@SiO2 excited by (b) 260 nm and (d) 360 nm.

(a) Excitation-emission plot of RHCDs@SiO2 monitored at phosphorescence mode. (b) The Phos. emission spectra and (c) CIE coordinate of the phosphorescence of RHCDs@SiO2 (EX: 240 nm). (d) Time-resolved decay curve of RHCDs@SiO2 monitored at EM: 460 nm and EX: 250 nm.

(a) The Phos. excitation spectra, (b) emission spectra (under excited by 240 nm) of CK@SiO2 and RHCDs@SiO2. (c) The time-resolved decay curves of CK@SiO2 (monitored at EM: 441 nm and EX: 251 nm) and RHCDs@SiO2 (monitored at EM: 460 nm and EX: 250 nm). EPR spectra (d) and g-factor maps (e) of CK@SiO2 and RHCDs@SiO2 (under dark 0 min conditions). (f) Schematic diagram showing energy level structure of RHCDs@SiO2.
The possible mechanism for electron trap-induced ultra-long afterglow lifetimes of RHCDs@SiO2.

Confocal images of HeLa cells cultured with RHCDs@SiO2 (0.1 mg/mL, 12 h), bright field (a) and (d), fluorescence images (b) and (e), and Phos. images (c) and (f) (delay: 8.5–12 ns), collecting channel: 415–470 nm and 470–550 nm.
Advanced optical anti-counterfeiting technology and information encryption applications. The bauhinia and SCAU patterns under (a), (c) 254 nm and (b), (d) 365 nm under UV lamp “on” and “off” states.
The diagram of the preparation procedures for RHCDs@SiO2.

head.ce:title: Multiple confinement-limited activation and defect effect co-triggered the ultra-long lifetime of carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National R&D Center for Se-rich Agricultural Products Processing, Hubei Engineering Research Center for Deep Processing of Green Se-rich Agricultural Products, School of Modern Industry for Selenium Science and Engineering, Wuhan Polytechnic University, Wuhan 430023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National R&D Center for Se-rich Agricultural Products Processing
Hubei Engineering Research Center for Deep Processing of Green Se-rich Agricultural Products
School of Modern Industry for Selenium Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National R&D Center for Se-rich Agricultural Products Processing, Hubei Engineering Research Center for Deep Processing of Green Se-rich Agricultural Products, School of Modern Industry for Selenium Science and Engineering, Wuhan Polytechnic University, Wuhan 430023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Utilizing the synergistic multiple confinement-defect effect, achieved the longest CDs afterglow lifetime to date in SiO2 matrix at 7.76 s.

The luminescence process of carbon dots (CDs) is typically influenced by spin-forbidden transition, triplet exciton quenching, and non-radiative transition. Thus, it faces great challenges to achieve efficient and ultra-long lifetime afterglow for CDs. The commonly employed strategy to obtain efficient afterglow performance of CDs is to encapsulate CDs into a rigid matrix to protect the afterglow signal from quenching. Generally, the ultra-long lifetime and excellent luminescent properties of inorganic RTP (room temperature phosphorescence) nanomaterials attribute to the slow release of electrons trapped in defect states of the surfaces. This capture-release mechanism has advanced afterglow lifetime of CDs towards longer levels via the design of defect states. Anchoring CDs onto a rigid matrix can preserve the rigid structure for the restrictive effect, and also can create structural defects. Herein, as-prepared RHCDs@SiO2 composite can exhibit an ultra-long afterglow lifetime of 7.76 s, which is the highest value achieved for CDs in silica matrix to date. This composite is also successfully applied in anti-counterfeiting and cell imaging fields. The synergistic multiple confinement effect and defect effect from the composite provides a universal strategy to design and achieve the ultra-long afterglow lifetime of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterial with ultra-long lifetime properties such as room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) or thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) have the advantage of long luminescence time and high signal-to-noise ratio in the time dimension [1–4]. As a new type of photoluminescence nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) not only have the advantages of bright luminescence and tunable emission wavelength, but also can demonstrate excellent photostability, high biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and ease availability [5–7]. CDs hold tremendous potentials in biomedical, optoelectronic devices, sensing, anti-counterfeiting, multi-level information encryption, and laser devices fields [8–16]. However, due to spin-forbidden transition effects, triplet excitons can be easily quenched in the presence of water or oxygen, and non-radiative transition process [17,18], causing the lifetime to be shortened and the low quantum efficiency.

To address the shortcomings, stabilizing and protecting triplet excitons, enhancing intersystem crossing (ISC) efficiency, and suppressing non-radiative processes have been actively employed [19–21]. Researchers generally believe that the multi-confinement effect of the matrix contributes to activating and enhancing the long afterglow of CDs, and it can be achieved by establishing a self-protective structure for CDs relying on polymer chains (such as PVA [19,22,23], PAM [24,25], etc.) or polymer-like structures, or combining CDs with rigid matrices (boric acid [26–28], Al2O3 [29], cyanuric acid [30], etc.) to construct multi-confinement structures (zeolite [31–33], SiO2 [34,35], etc.). These approaches disperse and confine CDs within a rigid matrix, preventing π-π stacking quenching caused by direct contact between carbon cores. Simultaneously, the rigid matrix restricts the vibrational motion of surface functional groups on CDs and reduces the energy loss of non-luminous processes in the process of non-radiative transformation, thus improving the afterglow performance. In addition, introducing heteroatoms (N, P, F, etc.) [7,36,37] into the system can enhance the spin–orbit coupling (SOC) between S1 and T1 states, as a result of the efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) processes and highly efficient RTP performance.

Notably, the ultra-long lifetime luminescence of inorganic nanomaterials originates from the slow release of captured charge carriers in traps (defects). This capture-recapture mechanism has also inspired the use of defects to favor the stabilization of triple excitons and to extend the afterglow lifetime of CDs [38,39]. In general, introducing non-native components (such as CDs or metal ions) into regular matrices may motivate the structural defect while preserving the constraint effect of rigid structures. Lu’s group achieved an 800 ms long RTP lifetime by inducing defect formation using Zn2+ in layered double hydroxides (LDHs), benefiting from the trap capture-recapture mechanism in the prepared composite material (Zn-CDs-LDH) [40]. He et al. anchored the CDs in a silica matrix to create traps that capture electrons, resulting in an afterglow lifetime of 1.76 s, which is due to the gradual release of electrons [41]. Han et al. constructed a trap state by introducing boron elements into nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. An ultra-long-lived dark blue RTP of 6.47 s was obtained because the defect traps and stores the excited electrons before releasing them slowly [38]. Song et al. by introducing CDs into a zinc aluminate matrix, the CDs@ZnAl2O4 composite material benefited from the constraint effect of the matrix and energy transfer of inorganic defect states, exhibiting the afterglow lifetime of 1.05 s [39]. Therefore, the rigid matrices play a crucial role in the efficient afterglow performance of CDs. Firstly, the main function of rigid matrices can suppress the vibration of surface functional groups on CDs. This suppression effect helps to reduce the probability of non-radiative transitions, thereby enhancing the luminescence efficiency of CDs [42,43]. The non-radiative transitions refer to the release of energy in the form of heat rather than photons. Reducing this transition means more energy can be converted into visible light, so the luminescence performance of CDs can be enhanced. Simultaneously, defect structures play a role in the capture and release of triplet excitons in CDs [40]. This mechanism extends the duration for triplet excitons returning to the ground state. Triplet excitons are excited-state electrons, and their lifetime directly affects the duration of afterglow [44]. By capturing and releasing through defect structures, the lifetime of triplet excitons is prolonged, thus the afterglow lifetime can be extended. In conclusion, the efficient afterglow performance of CDs relies on the multiple confinement effects of rigid matrices, and benefits from the construction of appropriate defects within the matrix. Rigid matrices enhance luminescence efficiency by suppressing non-radiative transitions, and defect structures improve afterglow performance through prolonging the lifetime of triplet excitons. This synergistic effect plays an important role in extending the afterglow lifetime of CDs-based nanomaterials.

In this study, the rice husk carbon dots (RHCDs) were firstly prepared before mixed with tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) to form SiO2 gel through a one-step hydrothermal method, and then calcined to obtain RHCDs@SiO2 composites. With the aid of highly rigid 3D network structure from the SiO2 matrix, and the stable Si-C and Si-O-C covalent bonds, the luminescent properties of the nanomaterial were activated. Additionally, the introduction of RHCDs caused local disruption of the original ordered structure of the SiO2 matrix, creating a great number of oxygen defects in the matrix surrounding the CDs. The presence of these defect states induced a capture-release process for triplet excitons, as a result of an ultra-long afterglow lifetime for CD-based composites. Under the excitation wavelength of 250 nm and emission wavelength of 460 nm, an ultra-long blue afterglow of 7.76 s was achieved, which is currently the highest value for CDs in the SiO2 matrix. After exposure to a 254 nm UV lamp, these CD-based composites can emit an afterglow transition from blue to cyan, with a duration of >27 s. Under the illumination of a 365 nm UV lamp, these composites can show the bright green afterglow. Based on the excitation-dependence behavior, ultra-long afterglow lifetime, quasi-nanospheres (16 nm) characteristic, low cytotoxicity, and stable luminescent performance in aqueous solution, these CD-based composites can be successfully applied in multi-level information encryption, anti-counterfeiting, and cellular imaging.

As shown in Scheme 1 , reaction raw materials were mixed with 20 mL of H2O before stirring for 4 h to form a clear solution. Next, the mixture was loaded into a 50 mL PTFE lined stainless steel reactor for the reaction of 2 h at 200 °C to form an encapsulated complex of RHCDs and SiO2 gel. At this stage, the SiO2 gel structure is loose and contains a large amount of water, so it does not exhibit phosphorescent properties. The complex was then dried and calcined at 600 °C for 1.5 h to obtain RHCDs@SiO2. Due to the large number of strong Si-C covalent bonds formed between RHCD and silica, as well as the confinement within the dense silicon-oxygen structure, the complex exhibited long-lasting phosphorescence. To prepare CK@SiO2 (CK: Calvin Klein) sample, which was without the introduction of RHCDs, the operational procedures remained the same as shown in the Supporting Information.

The structural characterization of the prepared RHCDs@SiO2 is shown in Fig. 1 , Fig. 2 , and Fig. S1. Fig. 1a presents the high-resolution SEM image, revealing the quasi-spherical morphology of RHCDs@SiO2 nanospheres with a particle size distribution of ∼16 nm. The TEM image also confirms the quasi-spherical shape (Fig. 1b). In the HRTEM image, the obtained CDs have the size of ∼2 nm (Fig. 1c and 1d). Additionally, HRTEM image (Fig. S1) demonstrates that CDs were successfully embedded in SiO2 matrix, and lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which attributes to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [45]. As shown in Fig. S2, the HRTEM image of CK@SiO2 clearly observes the distribution of carbon dot particles with a particle size of about 2.6 nm, whose 0.21 nm lattice stripe corresponds to the (100) crystalline surface of graphitic carbon. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurement was further conducted on RHCDs@SiO2. The broad band observed at 3212–3567 cm−1 corresponds to O-H bonds [46]. The band located at 1510–1716 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CO [47]. The bands observed at 978–1356 cm−1 and 754–872 cm−1 are assigned to the Si-O-C and Si-C covalent bonds, respectively [48]. The band observed at 415–515 cm−1 is attributed to Si-O covalent bonds as shown in Fig. 1e. The FTIR results indicate a close connection between RHCDs and theSiO2 matrix through Si-O-C and Si-C covalent bonds. The peak signal for the Si-C covalent bond became more pronounced after calcination. This covalent bonding interaction contributes to the formation of a rigid stabilizing structure within the RHCDs and silica network, which helps to activate the afterglow luminescence of the material. The XRD curves showed a broad peak near 22°, indicating that the phase of the complex is amorphous (Fig. 1f).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis further examined the chemical structure of the material. RHCDs@SiO2 primarily consists of three elements: C, O, and Si, having the amount of 9.62 %, 32.70 %, and 57.68 %, respectively. The peaks observed at 284.8 eV, 533.5 eV, and 103.9 eV correspond to the C1s, O1s, and Si2p binding energy, respectively (Fig. 2a). The high-resolution C1s spectrum reveals peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.8 eV, which can be attributed to C–C/CC, C–O, and CO covalent bonds, respectively (Fig. 2b) [49]. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum, the peak at 532.9 eV corresponds to CO bonds, and the peak at 533.7 eV corresponds to Si-O covalent bonds (Fig. 2c) [50]. The high-resolution Si2p spectrum shows the peaks at 102.9 eV, 103.6 eV, and 104.2 eV, which are assigned to Si-C, Si-O-C, and SiOx bonds, respectively (Fig. 2d) [45]. These results indicate that RHCDs have been successfully embedded into SiO2 matrix and firmly bonded together through rigid covalent bonds such as Si-C and Si-O-C, resulting in the multiple confinement effect. The dense silicon-oxygen network structure and stable covalent bond interactions provide structural constraints that activate the strong luminescent performance of RHCDs@SiO2.

Maintaining other experimental conditions unchanged, 50, 75, 125, and 150 µL of CDs solutions were added. The samples obtained from the preparation were 50-RHCDs@SiO2, 75-RHCDs@SiO2, 125-RHCDs@SiO2, and 150-RHCDs@SiO2. As shown in Fig. S3, the lifetimes of these four samples were 5.69, 6.11, 7.09, and 7.04 s respectively. It can be seen that the lifetimes first increased and then decreased with the increase of the concentration of the CDs. The lifetime of RHCDs@SiO2 with the addition of 100 µL was 7.76 s. Therefore, the addition of 100 µL of CDs was chosen for the study. As shown in Fig. S4, keeping the other experimental conditions unchanged, the lifetimes of a series of samples prepared by calcination at 500, 550, and 650 ℃ were 5.19, 6.07, and 6.63 s. However, the RHCDs@SiO2 prepared by addition at 600 ℃ had a lifetime of 7.76 s, and therefore, 600 ℃ was chosen as the calcination condition. RHCDs@SiO2 powder exhibits intense blue fluorescence under the source of 254 nm UV lamp. After the excitation is cut off, the powder shows a transition from cyan to blue phosphorescence, maintaining phosphorescent emission for a considerably long period of >27 s (Fig. 3 a). Under the excitation wavelength of 260 nm, the powder has a strong and sharp phosphorescent emission peak at ∼460 nm, with two shoulders at 440 nm and 500 nm (Fig. 3b), indicating RHCDs@SiO2 has the multi-emissive nature and excitation dependence property. Further studies confirmed the presence of excitation dependence in RHCDs@SiO2, as it exhibits bright blue fluorescence under 360 nm UV lamp irradiation and shows green phosphorescence after the excitation source turned off (Fig. 3c). The phosphorescent spectrum under 360 nm excitation displays a broad and intense emission peak at 500 nm (Fig. 3d), which corresponds to the captured green luminescence. As shown in Fig. S5, the peak at 203 nm is attributed to the characteristic absorption of the conjugated structure of aromatic carbon core. The absorption band at 227 nm is caused by the n-π* electronic transition. The absorption band at 275 nm corresponds to the π-π* electronic transition. The UV absorption peaks of CK@SiO2 and RHCDs@SiO2 are similar, while the absorption intensity of RHCDs@SiO2 is slightly stronger, which can be attributed to the introduction of RHCDs enhancing the UV absorption.

The excitation-emission plot of RHCDs@SiO2 monitored at fluorescence mode (Fig. S6) and the fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. S7), which exhibits a fluorescence emission peak at ∼390 nm under 240 nm excitation. Fig. 4 a shows the excitation-emission plot of RHCDs@SiO2, revealing the optimal excitation wavelength should be around 250 nm and the emission centre is around 460 nm. The phosphorescence emission spectrum indicates a noticeable excitation dependency relationship for RHCDs@SiO2, with the sharp and strong emission peak at 460 nm gradually disappearing and shifting towards 500 nm as the gradual increase of the excitation wavelength (Fig. 4b). According to the CIE (Commission International d′Éclairage) 1931 coordinate diagram, the afterglow color coordinate for RHCDs@SiO2 is (0.172, 0.200), locating in the blue-cyan region (Fig. 4c). The decay curves of the time-resolved phosphorescence spectra conform well to the results of the tri-exponential fitting function as shown in Fig. 4d, and the average lifetime can be calculated based on the following equation for the decay components:
τ
avg
=
∑
α i
τ i 2
/
∑
α i
τ i

The calculated lifetime is 7.76 s for RHCDs@SiO2, and the value is the highest one for the afterglow lifetime of CDs in silica matrix so far. The contribution values for τ1, τ2, and τ3 are 0.016, 3.168, and 11.369 s, respectively. However, the phosphorescence quantum yield of RHCDs@SiO2 was only 6.09 % (Fig. S8).

In order to further investigate the mechanism to achieve the long phosphorescence lifetime of RHCDs@SiO2, it was introduced that a control group CK@SiO2 without the addition of RHCDs. As shown in Fig. S9 and Fig. S10, the excitation-emission plot at fluorescence mode was monitored and the fluorescence emission spectra of CK@SiO2 were also measured under the excitation source of 220–340 nm, in which an obvious peak at ∼390 nm occurred after excited by 240 nm. However, the excitation-emission plot of CK@SiO2 shows two optimal excitation wavelengths at around 250 nm and 275 nm, and a luminescence center located at ∼440 nm (Fig. S11). However, the emission pattern of CK@SiO2 is not well consistent with that of RHCDs@SiO2 as shown in Fig. S12. Fig. S13 indicates that the CIE coordinate for CK@SiO2 under the excitation wavelength of 240 nm is (0.173, 0.181), which is different from that of RHCDs@SiO2 sample. According to the fitting formulas, the lifetime of CK@SiO2 is 4.89 s (Fig. S14), which is much shorter than that of RHCDs@SiO2 with the ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime of 7.76 s. As shown in Fig. S15, the fluorescence lifetimes of CK@SiO2 and RHCDs@SiO2 were 28.78 and 67.49 ns, respectively. As shown in Fig. S16, the peak fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions of the sample were 385 and 460 nm, respectively, under the excitation conditions of 80 K, 240 nm. ΔEst of RHCDs@SiO2 was calculated to be 0.53 eV according to Eq:
Δ E s t =
( 1240 /
λ
F l u o .
)
-
( 1240 /
λ
P h o s .
)
. As shown in Fig. S17, the afterglow lifetimes of 6.56, 7.53, and 6.22 s were maintained in concentrated H2SO4, concentrated HCl, and NaOH (pH = 14) solutions, respectively, which proves that the material is able to maintain the superb afterglow performance in extreme external condition.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, by comparing the phosphorescence excitation spectra of RHCDs@SiO2 and CK@SiO2, it can be observed that CK@SiO2 indeed exhibits two obvious peaks at 251 nm and 280 nm. Moreover, RHCDs@SiO2 only shows one optimal excitation peak at 251 nm. Under the common excitation wavelength of 240 nm, CK@SiO2 exhibits an optimal emission wavelength of ∼441 nm. However, RHCDs@SiO2 displays an optimal emission peak at ∼460 nm having the feature of a sharp and strong emission peak and exhibiting shoulder peaks at 440 nm and 500 nm (Fig. 5b). The fitting calculation results for the lifetime are shown in Fig. 5c, where RHCDs@SiO2 demonstrates an ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime of 7.76 s. In order to further reveal the mechanism for the ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime from RHCDs@SiO2, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra of both RHCDs@SiO2 and CK@SiO2 were recorded to characterize the distribution of oxygen vacancies (VO). As depicted in Fig. 5d, RHCDs@SiO2 shows a strong EPR signal, while CK@SiO2 only exhibits weak peak values. The presence of a strong signal at g = 2.003 indicates the abundance of oxygen vacancies in RHCDs@SiO2 (Fig. 5e), whereas CK@SiO2 only displays weak signals. Through quantitative EPR analysis, it was determined that the unit volume spin concentration (spins/mm3), molar concentration of spin electrons (M), and total spin number (spins) in RHCDs@SiO2 are 8.235e + 11, 1.368e-06, and 2.271e + 13, respectively. However, in CK@SiO2, the unit volume spin concentration (spins/mm3), molar concentration of spin electrons (M), and total spin number (spins) are 1.381e + 11, 2.293e-07, and 3.808e + 12, respectively (Table S1). These results indicate the presence of a large number of oxygen vacancies in RHCDs@SiO2, with the oxygen vacancy concentration being significantly higher than that in CK@SiO2. The abundance of oxygen vacancies in RHCDs@SiO2 can act as electron traps, playing a capture-release mechanism during the transition of triplet excitons, thereby greatly prolonging the returning time of excitons to the ground state, and achieving a significant prolongation of the phosphorescence lifetime. The corresponding energy level structure of RHCDs@SiO2 is shown in Fig. 5f.

Based on the above results, we reasonably hypothesize the composite structure between RHCDs and SiO2 matrix and deduce the mechanism behind the generation of the ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 6 , RHCDs are embedded in the network structure of SiO2 and tightly bonded through rigid covalent bonds such as Si-C and Si-O-C, as a result of the multi-confinement effect that activates the excellent phosphorescent performance of RHCDs@SiO2. With the introduction of RHCDs, the regular SiO2 structure surrounding them is locally disrupted, causing the generation of oxygen vacancies (defects). The abundant presence of these defects induces the ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime of RHCDs@SiO2. (1) Electrons in the ground state are excited by ultraviolet light and reach the S1 state. (2) During the process of electron returning from the S1 state to the ground state, some electrons are trapped by the defects. (3) Through quantum tunneling effects of the defects or external energy perturbation, the trapped electrons are triggered to be released, and some electrons jump to the T1 state. (4) Electrons in the T1 state transition get back to the ground state and release excess energy as form of light, resulting in RTP. Due to the presence of numerous oxygen vacancies in RHCDs@SiO2, the probabilities of (2) and (3) processes are greatly increased, and the capture-release process of the electrons is significantly delayed, thereby triggering the generation of the 7.76 s ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime. The proposal of this synergistic mechanism provides a novel research perspective to design and construct CDs with ultra-long phosphorescence lifetimes.

MTT assay was performed on HeLa cells to assess the cytotoxicity of RHCDs@SiO2. As shown in Fig. S18, cell viability remained above 85 % after cultured with RHCDs@SiO2 in the range of 0.0 to 0.8 mg/mL for 48 h, indicating relatively low biotoxicity of RHCDs@SiO2 and suitability for biomedical imaging. Thus, HeLa cells were cultured with RHCDs@SiO2 (0.1 mg/mL) for 12 h, then washed three times with PBS buffer solution, and their luminescent images were recorded by using confocal microscopy. Fig. 7 a and Fig. 7d show the morphology of HeLa cells under bright field, with distinct cell edges and good adherence, indicating good cell viability after co-cultured with RHCDs@SiO2. Blue and green signals from Hela cells can be observed in the ranges of 415–470 nm and 470–550 nm, respectively (Fig. 7b and Fig. 7e). After a time delay of 8.5–12 ns, the signal intensity decreased, but the cell morphology was still kept well (Fig. 7c and Fig. 7f). Furthermore, RHCDs@SiO2 particles were homogeneously distributed in HeLa cells, demonstrating its potential in phosphorescence bioimaging. In dependence of excellent phosphorescent performance of RHCDs@SiO2, the applications in anti-counterfeiting and advanced information encryption were conducted. As shown in Fig. 8 , two patterns, namely “Bauhinia” and “SCAU,” were designed using this powdery RHCDs@SiO2. Under 254 nm UV lamp irradiation, both “Bauhinia” (Fig. 8a) and “SCAU” (Fig. 8c) exhibit blue fluorescence, and maintain a bright and sustained cyan/blue phosphorescence emission after turning off the UV lamp, with clear visibility of the patterns. Under 365 nm excitation, both “Bauhinia” (Fig. 8b) and “SCAU” (Fig. 8d) display blue fluorescence, and exhibit green phosphorescence after the light source was cut off. These results indicate the broad application prospects of RHCDs@SiO2 in the field of anti-counterfeiting and advanced information encryption.

In summary, it is proposed that a strategy to achieve an ultra-long afterglow lifetime of CDs by utilizing the synergistic effect of matrix multiple confinement-defect induction. The design concept involves using dense and 3D-network-structured SiO2 as the matrix to activate the afterglow emission of CDs through multiple confinement effects. RHCDs are introduced as the luminescent centers, and the incorporation of CDs generates numerous oxygen vacancies. By utilizing these defects as electron trapping centers and relying on the “capture-release” process, the duration for electrons to return to the ground state is prolonged, resulting in the extended afterglow lifetime. The assembled RHCDs@SiO2 system exhibits an astonishing afterglow lifetime of 7.76 s, which was the longest value achieved for CDs in SiO2 matrices so far. Furthermore, the system also demonstrates excellent biocompatibility, and the extended afterglow lifetime offers tremendous potential in cellular imaging. Therefore, the proposed synergistic strategy contributes to design the CD-based materials with prolonged afterglow lifetimes.

Yimeng Luo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Qin Jiang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Jinkun Liu: Visualization, Software. Feiyan Huang: Visualization, Formal analysis. Xuelin Liao: Visualization, Methodology. Jianle Zhuang: Visualization, Validation, Funding acquisition. Chaofan Hu: Visualization. Mingtao Zheng: Visualization. Bingfu Lei: Visualization. Yingliang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jiangling He: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12174119 and No. 52172142) and the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation, China (2022A1515011958). Wuhan Talents –Outstanding Young Talents Project Funding of Jiangling He.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.156781.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
